findings.title: Library API documented via type annotations but no formal machine-readable spec
findings.description: The SDK exposes a well-documented Python API through sentry_sdk/__init__.py and sentry_sdk/api.py with 40+ public functions/classes. Type annotations are comprehensive and the package ships py.typed for PEP 561 compliance. External documentation at docs.sentry.io provides usage guides. No formal OpenAPI/AsyncAPI specification exists, which is expected for a library.
findings.gap: No formal machine-readable API specification file. For a library, type annotations serve this purpose.
findings.recommendation: Continue maintaining comprehensive type annotations. Consider generating a machine-readable API manifest for agent tool binding.

findings.title: DSN-based authentication lacks principal attribution
findings.description: The SDK authenticates to the Sentry backend exclusively via DSN (Data Source Name), which embeds a public key and project ID. The DSN is not a machine-identity credential in the IAM/OAuth sense — it is a project-scoped ingest token. There is no support for OAuth 2.0 client credentials, mTLS, or principal-attributed machine identity within the SDK itself.
findings.gap: DSN identifies a project, not a specific agent. No principal attribution in authentication.
findings.recommendation: Document patterns for agent identity attribution via tags and separate DSNs per agent identity.

findings.title: No scoped permission model within SDK
findings.description: The SDK authenticates to the Sentry backend using a single DSN string. There is no mechanism within the SDK itself for scoped permissions — the DSN grants full write access to a specific Sentry project. There are no IAM policies, role definitions, or permission scopes in the codebase.
findings.gap: No scoped permission model exists. Any consumer with the DSN can send any event type to the Sentry project.
findings.recommendation: Document recommended patterns for agent consumers: create dedicated Sentry projects per agent identity with separate DSNs.

findings.title: No action-level authorization within SDK
findings.description: The SDK provides no action-level authorization. All operations (capture_exception, capture_message, start_span, flush, etc.) are available to any consumer that has initialized the SDK with a valid DSN.
findings.gap: No ABAC or fine-grained RBAC exists within the library.
findings.recommendation: Document wrapper patterns for consuming applications to enforce action-level controls.

findings.title: No secrets management integration for DSN
findings.description: The SDK accepts the DSN as a string parameter to init() or reads it from the SENTRY_DSN environment variable. There is no integration with AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or any secrets management system within the SDK itself.
findings.gap: No secrets management integration. Consuming applications responsible for DSN security.
findings.recommendation: Document that DSNs should be injected via secrets-managed environment variables.

findings.title: No agent identity suspension mechanism in SDK
findings.description: The SDK has no concept of agent identity or identity suspension. The only authentication mechanism is the DSN string. While a DSN can be invalidated server-side by revoking the project key in Sentry, the SDK itself provides no API for identity lifecycle management, suspension, or revocation.
findings.gap: No programmatic API for identity suspension within the library.
findings.recommendation: Document DSN revocation as agent suspension pattern. Consider adding disable() method.

findings.title: No proactive client-side rate limiting
findings.description: The SDK implements client-side rate limiting in response to 429 responses from the Sentry server but does not enforce proactive rate limits on the caller. An agent invoking capture_exception() in a tight loop would generate unbounded outbound HTTP requests until the Sentry backend returns 429 responses.
findings.gap: No configurable client-side rate limit to prevent agent loops from overwhelming the outbound queue.

findings.title: No automated breaking-change detection in CI
findings.description: The SDK uses semantic versioning (v2.56.0) with a comprehensive CHANGELOG.md. The checkouts/data-schemas git submodule references event schema definitions. py.typed is shipped. However, there is no automated breaking-change detection in CI.
findings.gap: No automated API surface change detection in CI pipeline.
findings.recommendation: Add automated API surface check (e.g., griffe) to CI.

findings.title: SDK internal diagnostics use standard logging (appropriate for library)
findings.description: The SDK IS an observability library providing distributed tracing and OpenTelemetry integration. Its own internal diagnostics use standard Python logging (not structured JSON with correlation IDs), which is appropriate for a library.
findings.gap: SDK internal diagnostics lack structured logging, but this is expected for a library.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Internal diagnostics via standard logging is appropriate.
